parentQuestion: A single cone photoreceptor often connects to a single bipolar cell and a single ganglion cell, while as many as 100 rods will converge to a single ganglion cell.

scaffolds.q: Photoreceptors connect to ganglion cells through intermediate cells (bipolar, etc.).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Photoreceptor → bipolar cell → ganglion cell → optic nerve.

scaffolds.q: Ganglion cells are the retinal neurons whose axons form the optic nerve.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Ganglion cell axons bundle to exit the eye as the optic nerve.

scaffolds.q: 'Convergence' refers to many inputs feeding into fewer outputs.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Multiple rods → one ganglion cell = high convergence.

scaffolds.q: Cones have LOW convergence (few cones per ganglion cell).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! In fovea, often 1 cone → 1 bipolar → 1 ganglion cell. Private line!

scaffolds.q: Rods have HIGH convergence (many rods per ganglion cell).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! ~100 rods may converge on one ganglion cell. Party line!

scaffolds.q: Low convergence = high spatial resolution (you know exactly which cone detected light).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Private lines preserve location information. Sharp acuity.

scaffolds.q: High convergence = high sensitivity (sum many weak signals) but low resolution.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Many rods pooled can detect dim light, but you can't tell WHICH rod saw it.

scaffolds.q: Cone pathways have 1:1 convergence for acuity; rod pathways converge ~100:1 for sensitivity.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This is the resolution-sensitivity tradeoff between cone and rod vision.

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - cones have ~1:1 wiring, rods converge ~100:1.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Low cone convergence = high acuity (daytime detail). High rod convergence = high sensitivity (dim light detection) but coarse resolution.
